d: Natural charm and magnetic charisma, especially the effortless ability to attract romantic interest without really trying.
ex: She walked into the party and started talking to everyone — the girl has serious rizz.

d: An exclamation of shock or admiration at someone with a very pronounced backside or an impressive physique.
ex: He scrolled past the photo and just said 'gyatt' before double-tapping.

d: A jaw-positioning technique practiced with the goal of reshaping facial structure over time, popularized heavily on social media.
ex: He's been mewing for six months and swears his jawline has changed.

d: The practice of optimizing one's physical appearance through every possible means, from skincare and haircuts to posture and diet.
ex: His whole TikTok feed is looksmaxxing content now — serums, bone-smashing, the works.

d: Completely outclassing or dominating someone else in looks, status, or performance just by being present.
ex: He walked in and mogged everyone in the room without even trying.

d: A self-reliant, independent person who succeeds outside of conventional social hierarchies without needing approval from others.
ex: He left the group chat, started his own business, and never looked back — total sigma move.

d: The act of taking a portion of someone else's food or snacks, named after a streamer known for stealing bites from friends.
ex: I barely sat down before he hit me with the fanum tax and took half my fries.

d: Excessive, over-the-top praise or admiration for someone, often to the point of being embarrassing or sycophantic.
ex: The comments are pure glazing — every reply is calling him a genius for a mediocre take.

d: Delusional in a playful sense — believing something wildly optimistic about oneself or a situation, especially in romance.
ex: She thinks her celebrity crush noticed her fan account. She is delulu and she knows it.

d: Used as a nonsense intensifier or descriptor, often ironically, to describe something weird, random, or over the top.
ex: That presentation was giving skibidi energy — chaotic but somehow it worked.

d: Acting in a robotic, predictable, or unthinking way, as if you were a background character in a video game.
ex: He just stood there nodding while everyone argued — total NPC behavior.

d: The state of one's mind becoming increasingly consumed by niche internet content, memes, and obscure references.
ex: I've watched so much weird TikTok that I think I have genuine brainrot at this point.

d: To execute something with exceptional style, confidence, or skill; a general expression of approval for someone's look or performance.
ex: She walked into that interview and absolutely slayed — they offered her the job on the spot.

d: Honestly; without exaggeration — used to emphasize that what you're saying is completely true.
ex: That was the best pizza I've ever eaten, no cap.

d: Short for 'for real,' used to agree strongly with something or to stress sincerity.
ex: That class was impossible, fr — nobody passed the first exam.

d: Describing food that tastes exceptionally good; occasionally extended to other things that are excellent.
ex: Your mom's jollof rice is bussin — I need that recipe.

d: Subtly; to a moderate degree — used to admit something without fully committing to the statement.
ex: I lowkey love that show even though I talked trash about it for months.

d: Openly and obviously; the emphatic opposite of lowkey — used when you're not hiding your feelings at all.
ex: I highkey need a nap right now, I cannot focus on anything.

d: Affects you in a deeper or more meaningful way than usual, often tied to a specific mood or circumstance.
ex: Driving home alone at midnight with that album playing just hits different.

d: Perfectly grasped what was needed and delivered exactly that — often used as praise for someone's look, effort, or execution.
ex: Her Halloween costume this year? She absolutely understood the assignment.

d: The attitude of someone who acts like the protagonist of their own story — confident, self-focused, and unbothered.
ex: She wore a bright red trench coat to the grocery store. Full main character energy.

d: A distinct phase or period of one's life characterized by a specific mood, attitude, or focus.
ex: I deleted all social media and started hiking — fully in my healing era.

d: A romantic or emotionally intimate arrangement that isn't quite a relationship but is more than just friendship — with no clear labels.
ex: We've been texting every day for four months and I still don't know what we are. Classic situationship.

d: Describing something that occupies your mind constantly without any effort — you can't stop thinking about it.
ex: That embarrassing thing I said in 2017 still lives rent free in my head.

d: An impromptu assessment of someone's mood, energy, or general attitude in a given moment.
ex: Before we bring him on the trip, we need to do a vibe check — last time was a disaster.

d: It has the energy, aesthetic, or feel of something — often followed by what it evokes.
ex: That whole outfit is giving old Hollywood glamour and I'm obsessed with it.

d: A person who seems to lack independent thought or personality, acting on autopilot like a non-player character in a video game.
ex: He just agreed with everything the boss said — he's such an NPC at work.

d: Looking exceptionally good, particularly referring to a well-defined waist or an overall polished, put-together appearance.
ex: After six weeks of working out, her waist is snatched — she looks incredible.

d: Performed something so perfectly that there is nothing left to critique — an absolute flawless execution.
ex: Her verse on that track? She ate and left no crumbs. Best feature of the year.

d: Confident in one's own opinions or identity without caring about others' approval; authentically, unapologetically yourself.
ex: He wore the same shirt to every event and didn't care what anyone thought. Based.

d: Mediocre; average at best — used to dismiss something as underwhelming or not worth the hype.
ex: I waited in line for an hour for that burger and it was completely mid.

d: Suspicious or questionable — something or someone that doesn't seem right or seems to be hiding something.
ex: He logged off right when the topic came up. That's extremely sus.

d: When a reply to a post gets significantly more engagement than the original, implying the original was wrong or bad.
ex: He doubled down on that opinion and got ratioed so hard the replies are still going.

d: Go outside and interact with the real world — advice given to someone who has been too deep in internet culture.
ex: If you're spending three hours arguing about a fictional character's morality, please touch grass.

d: An encouragement to continue speaking your mind passionately, or a description of someone doing so without restraint.
ex: She went off in the meeting about the policy change and honestly? Good. Someone had to say it.

d: A win — something positive that happened or a good decision made.
ex: Got the refund, got an apology, and they upgraded my order for free. That's a W.

d: A loss — a failure, bad outcome, or embarrassing situation.
ex: Spent forty minutes cooking and then dropped the whole pan. That's an L.

d: Completely serious; without any joke or exaggeration — I mean exactly what I'm saying.
ex: I deadass forgot my own birthday was this week. Completely slipped my mind.

d: A lie or exaggeration — saying something that isn't true.
ex: He said he ran a 4.3 forty? That's cap — I've seen him run.

d: An expression of agreement, confirmation, or acknowledgment — similar to 'alright' or 'sounds good.'
ex: Meet me at seven? Bet, I'll be there.

d: An exclamation expressing disbelief, admiration, or exaggerated reaction to something impressive or shocking.
ex: He benched 315 on his first attempt. Sheesh.

d: Being or considered the greatest of all time — exceptional, legendary, irreplaceable.
ex: That performance was goated. Nobody else in the field even comes close.

d: Stylish clothing, accessories, and overall fashion sense — an impressive, eye-catching outfit or personal style.
ex: He stepped out of the car with full drip — designer from head to toe.

d: Exceptional; extremely good — applied to music, food, outfits, or almost anything that impresses.
ex: This playlist is straight fire — I haven't skipped a single track.

d: When a song sounds incredible and gets you moving involuntarily — it just hits.
ex: That new track from her slaps harder than anything she's put out before.

d: Bitter, upset, or resentful about something, often over a minor slight or perceived unfairness.
ex: He's been salty all week because he lost that bet and had to pay up.

d: Behaving in an excessive, dramatic, or over-the-top way — doing far more than the situation calls for.
ex: She brought a seven-layer cake to a casual hangout. She is so extra.

d: Someone who does excessive things for another person's attention or affection, often without any reciprocation.
ex: He bought her a gift after she texted him once. Classic simp behavior.

d: Social influence, fame, or internet popularity — the currency of relevance online.
ex: She only attends events for the clout at this point; she doesn't even like those people.

d: Showing off something you have — whether wealth, skill, looks, or achievements — to impress others.
ex: He posted his paycheck as a story. That's a weird flex but okay.

d: A nickname for someone who is entitled, demanding, and quick to speak to a manager over minor inconveniences.
ex: The Karen at table six complained about her steak being too steak-flavored.

d: The mood, atmosphere, or general feeling given off by a person, place, or situation.
ex: This coffee shop has such a good vibe — I could stay here all afternoon.

d: An emphatic punctuation word used to signal the end of a statement — no argument will be entertained.
ex: She is the most talented person in that office, periodt.

d: Used as a standalone acknowledgment that carries additional weight — suggesting the listener truly gets the deeper meaning.
ex: She told him to figure it out on his own. He said 'understood' and never texted back.

d: A dismissive response to outdated or out-of-touch opinions, especially from older generations resistant to change.
ex: He said social media isn't real life and she just responded 'OK boomer' and walked away.

d: Cutting off all contact with someone without explanation — calls, texts, and messages go completely unanswered.
ex: They were talking every day for two months and then he just started ghosting her.

d: An extremely devoted fan of a celebrity or public figure; also used as a verb meaning to admire or support intensely.
ex: She stans that band so hard she's seen them live fourteen times across three countries.

d: The social phenomenon of publicly withdrawing support from and calling out a person or brand for objectionable behavior.
ex: The backlash was immediate — cancel culture came for him within hours of the post going live.

d: Harmful, manipulative, or destructive in a relationship or dynamic — emotionally or socially damaging.
ex: That friendship was toxic — she always felt worse about herself after seeing him.

d: A significant positive transformation in one's appearance, confidence, or life circumstances over time.
ex: She went from awkward freshman to class president. The glow up was real.

d: Gossip, drama, or inside information about what's really going on with someone.
ex: Sit down, I have tea — apparently she quit on the spot and called out the whole department on her way out.

d: To share gossip or reveal dramatic insider information that others are eager to hear.
ex: She finally spilled the tea on why the two of them aren't speaking anymore.

d: Fear of missing out — the anxious feeling that something exciting is happening somewhere else without you.
ex: She checked Instagram the whole trip and spent every day with FOMO about events back home.

d: You only live once — used to justify doing something risky or spontaneous.
ex: He booked a one-way ticket to Portugal on a Tuesday. YOLO, he said.

d: Your close-knit group of friends who you regularly spend time with and can count on.
ex: The whole squad showed up at the airport to send her off — it was genuinely sweet.

d: Making subtle, indirect insults or negative comments about someone, often without naming them directly.
ex: She smiled at her and complimented her 'bravery' for wearing that — classic shade.

d: Proof or evidence — screenshots, messages, or other documentation that backs up a claim.
ex: She said it never happened, but we have receipts — every text is right here.

d: Aware of and attentive to social justice issues and systemic inequality — though the term has since become heavily politicized.
ex: He called the ad campaign woke for featuring a diverse cast, which told you everything about him.

d: Exciting, fun, and full of energy — applied to parties, events, or anything with a great atmosphere.
ex: That concert was absolutely lit from the first song to the encore.

d: As f**k — used as an intensifier to emphasize the degree of whatever adjective it follows.
ex: It is hot AF in here — can someone open a window?

d: A subtle or indirect insult directed at someone, often without them fully realizing they've been criticized.
ex: She said she liked 'effortless looks' while staring directly at her — full shade.

d: Mainstream, unoriginal, or predictably conventional in taste — following trends without any personal flair.
ex: Pumpkin spice everything in October is basic, and she fully embraces being basic.

d: Desperately seeking attention, validation, or romantic interest in an obvious or embarrassing way.
ex: He commented 'gorgeous' on every single one of her photos from the past two years. Thirsty.

d: To be honest — used to soften or introduce a candid opinion.
ex: TBH, I think the original was better. The sequel didn't need to exist.

d: A disclaimer meaning no criticism or judgment is intended in what's about to be said — purely observational.
ex: No tea no shade, but that color really doesn't work with her complexion.

d: Feeling grateful and fortunate; acknowledging something good that happened as a result of luck or circumstance.
ex: Found twenty dollars in my old jacket this morning. Feeling truly blessed.

d: The act of doing responsible, grown-up tasks — like paying bills, cooking meals, or making appointments — that feel surprisingly hard.
ex: I made a dentist appointment AND did laundry on the same day. Adulting wins.

d: A secondary job or income stream pursued alongside a main job, often based on a personal skill or passion.
ex: She makes more from her Etsy shop than her actual salary. The side hustle became the main hustle.

d: To achieve something smoothly and cleverly, often through charm or subtle manipulation rather than brute force.
ex: He finessed his way into VIP without a ticket and somehow left with three business cards.

d: In my opinion — used to flag that what follows is a personal view, not an objective statement.
ex: IMO, the third episode was the best in the whole series.

d: Greatest of all time — used to designate someone as the undisputed best in their field.
ex: She broke every record in the sport. She is the GOAT and it's not even a debate.

d: A photo or video posted specifically to attract attention and compliments, usually by showing off one's attractiveness.
ex: She posted that mirror selfie right after he unfollowed her. That was a calculated thirst trap.

d: Adorably tiny or small — used affectionately to describe something or someone impossibly cute and miniature.
ex: Look at this smol puppy wearing a sweater. I cannot handle it.

d: Perfectly styled or executed — on point in every detail, especially regarding appearance.
ex: Her eyebrows were on fleek every single day that semester. People asked her for tips.

d: A term of affection for a romantic partner or someone you find extremely attractive — short for 'before anyone else.'
ex: She posted a photo with him with just 'bae' as the caption back in 2015.

d: Extremely excited, energized, or intoxicated at a party or social event — ready to have a chaotic good time.
ex: The DJ dropped that song and everyone got turnt immediately.

d: Totally — an abbreviated, enthusiastic affirmation.
ex: She said totes every other sentence for an entire year and then just stopped one day.

d: Adorable — an abbreviated compliment for something impossibly cute or charming.
ex: The mini cupcakes at the baby shower were adorbs — tiny and perfectly decorated.

d: Amazing and fantastic — an enthusiastic but now somewhat dated expression of delight.
ex: Her birthday party was amazeballs. She said that unironically in 2012.

d: Executed with precision and quality — everything is exactly as it should be.
ex: Her presentation was on point: clear slides, confident delivery, and great data.

d: Stylish confidence and cool personal flair — an aura of effortless swagger in appearance or attitude.
ex: He had this natural swag that made even an old hoodie look intentional.

d: An aspirational group of friends or an activity shared among close friends that represents what you want for your own friend group.
ex: They all showed up in coordinated costumes. That is squad goals.

d: Ruthlessly bold, blunt, or brutal in a way that is impressive rather than cruel — unapologetically direct.
ex: She clapped back with one sentence and walked away. Absolutely savage.

d: Laughing my a** off — used to express genuine amusement, often typed but rarely spoken aloud anymore.
ex: He tripped walking into his own surprise party. LMAO is the only appropriate response.

d: Once a subtle qualifier, 'lowkey' has become so overused that it's starting to lose its meaning in everyday speech.
ex: Everything was lowkey whatever by the time the word hit mainstream ad copy.

d: Once fresh internet vocabulary, 'salty' has gone so mainstream that it now appears in corporate emails and dad jokes.
ex: When your uncle uses 'salty' correctly at Thanksgiving, you know the word has peaked.

d: A once-popular game of rating whether you'd pursue or reject someone romantically — now considered dated and low effort.
ex: Smash or pass polls were everywhere on Twitter for about two years and then everyone moved on.

d: Upset or emotionally activated by something — originally clinical language that became a sarcastic meme and then faded.
ex: She said she was triggered by his opinion on pineapple pizza. That joke is retired.

d: Rolling on the floor laughing — once the gold standard of internet hilarity, now almost never used unironically.
ex: He typed ROFL in the group chat and everyone else went silent. Somebody said 'what year is it?'

d: A spectacular and embarrassing failure, popular as a reaction label in early internet culture.
ex: Every bloopers compilation from 2010 was just called 'EPIC FAIL COMPILATION #47.'

d: A single-word reaction to someone's mistake, derived from early meme culture — now deeply dated.
ex: Someone replied 'fail' to her tweet and she felt like she had traveled back to 2009.

d: Doing something with extreme confidence and authority — a phrase that was everywhere briefly and then became a cliché.
ex: 'Like a boss' was in every motivational poster, mug, and phone case for about three years straight.

d: A dance move where you drop your head into the crook of your bent arm while pointing the other arm outward.
ex: He dabbed in his graduation photo in 2016 and now cannot look at it without cringing.

d: A hand gesture performed to acknowledge an awkward moment — both hands stacked, thumbs rotating outward.
ex: She did the awkward turtle at the wrong moment during dinner and nobody laughed.

d: Speaking the word 'hashtag' before a phrase in conversation instead of just saying the phrase — a brief and universally cringe trend.
ex: He said 'hashtag blessed' out loud at breakfast in 2014 and she never fully forgave him.

d: An attempt to make 'fetch' happen as slang for cool, originating from Mean Girls — famous for never catching on.
ex: She tried to use 'fetch' for an entire semester in high school. It never happened.

d: Actually saying the letters L-M-A-O out loud in conversation rather than typing them — an early 2010s habit that has since vanished.
ex: She said 'el-mao' in response to his joke and even he looked uncomfortable.

d: Once the pinnacle of appearance compliments, 'on fleek' became so overused in branding that it lost all meaning and was abandoned.
ex: When Denny's used 'on fleek' in a promotional tweet, the slang was declared officially dead.

d: The original 'period' used for emphasis before the 't' variant took over — still heard but increasingly replaced.
ex: She ended every sentence with 'period' for emphasis back in 2017 before 'periodt' existed.

d: Once a sharp compliment, this phrase has been applied so broadly and frequently that it now often reads as filler rather than praise.
ex: Every brand account used 'understood the assignment' by mid-2022 and the phrase hasn't recovered.

d: Still in circulation but appearing in enough think pieces and corporate content that it's losing its edge.
ex: A finance newsletter used 'rent free in your head' to describe interest rates and nobody laughed.

d: Viewing yourself as the protagonist of your life and acting with that level of intentionality and dramatic flair.
ex: She narrated her whole commute like she was in a film. Full main character moment.

d: Acting in an erratic, chaotic, or wildly irrational way — often used with admiration rather than alarm.
ex: She showed up to his office with a glitter cannon. Absolutely unhinged. Iconic.

d: A suffix added to describe a highly specific aesthetic or lifestyle niche, like cottagecore or dark academia.
ex: Her whole apartment is goblincore — dark wood, crystals, framed bugs. It works somehow.

d: Captured doing something wrong or embarrassing in high definition — undeniable proof of guilt or hypocrisy.
ex: He said he was studying, but someone posted a video of him at the mall. Caught in 4K.

d: The opposite of 'it's giving' — something is failing to produce the intended impression or energy.
ex: That poster design is not giving 'luxury brand' — it's giving clip art.

d: A state of pleasant mental blankness — used humorously when you have nothing insightful to say.
ex: She stared at the essay prompt for twenty minutes. No thoughts, head empty.

d: Correctly read the mood of a room or situation and responded perfectly to it.
ex: He showed up with snacks and no questions when she said she needed company. Understood the vibe.

d: An affectionate nickname for a partner, friend, or pet — cute and playful without being too serious.
ex: She calls her cat pookie and her boyfriend pookie and has never seen the need to differentiate.

d: Variant of 'it's giving' used to describe the vibe or energy something radiates.
ex: Her whole outfit? That's giving vintage Parisian — absolute cinema.

d: Specifically being in one's 'academic era' — a phase of focused study, self-improvement, and intellectual dedication.
ex: She turned off notifications and started going to the library daily. Deep in her academic era.

d: A period in someone's life where they stop being overly accommodating and start prioritizing themselves, sometimes ruthlessly.
ex: She blocked three people, quit a committee, and stopped replying to texts. Villain arc activated.

d: Describing something done with minimal care or thought — used to dismiss lazy content or half-hearted attempts.
ex: That apology was so low-effort it actually made things worse.

d: The glorification of relentless work and productivity as a personality trait and lifestyle, often at the expense of rest or relationships.
ex: He wore 'no days off' like a badge of honor — fully bought into hustle culture.
